label: no
cot_prefix: Okay, let's see. I need to figure out which of the four statements is true according to the passage. Let's go through the passage again to make sure I understand it correctly. The passage starts by saying that the British government is reviewing the use of lotteries to allocate school places because more than 20% of children are failing to get into their first-choice schools in some parts of the country. So, right there, that might be a key point for one of the options. The struggle for secondary school places has reached record levels this year, causing anxiety. A report from 43 local authorities says in many areas, up to a fifth of children face disappointment. Families in London are the hardest hit. So "up to a fifth" is 20%, so over one in five children in some areas. That might relate to option B. Then, the article mentions that the recession is making parents consider state education instead of private, leading to more applications in over a third of local authorities. So applications are increasing, not falling, which relates to option D (which says the number is falling), so D is probably false. In Richmond-upon-Thames, 62% got their first-choice school, down from 64% last year. Tower Hamlet had 71.1%, Leeds and Warwickshire 85%, Derby 81%, and others like Wiltshire, Stockport, and Lincolnshire 89%. So in some areas, the success rate is higher, but in others, it's lower.
